words.cultural_note: Basic and friendly way to ask someone how they are doing this phrased is used often in everyday conversations.
The standard response to Kedu. In Igbo culture, wellbeing is communal — when you say you are fine, you implicitly include your household and those around you.
This is a general greeting used to show respect. It’s polite and appropriate in most social settings.

Your chi is the personal spirit guiding your unique path, chosen in agreement with you before birth. It's why so many Igbo names begin with 'Chi' — Chinwendu, Chioma — each one a small declaration of that bond.
The Supreme Creator God, source of all chi and ultimate moral authority.

Also called Ani, Ala is the earth goddess who governs morality, fertility, and the harvest. Offenses against the land — incest, suicide, certain crimes — are considered offenses against Ala directly, making her the moral foundation of Igbo society.
General term for spiritual beings, including ancestors, used in ceremonies and to describe the unseen world that interacts constantly with the living.
The patrilineal kinship network — far broader than a nuclear family — bound by mutual obligation, shared land, and collective responsibility in disputes or celebrations
Carries specific ceremonial duties and is often seen as a stabilizing figure in the family, sometimes with authority over certain rites even above younger brothers.
Also called Opara in some dialects, the first son inherits headship of the household and is responsible for continuing family rites, including ancestral worship and dispute mediation.
